segments.text: The next sutra is 6.5.45. The Buddha said,
Mangs is poverty a woeful thing for an immoral worldly person.
Surely Lord, the monk replied, and when a man is poor,
needy in straits, he gets into debt. Is that woeful too?
Surely Lord, and when he gets into debt he borrows.
Is that woeful too? Surely Lord, and when the bill
falls due, he pays naught, and they press him. Is that woeful too?
Surely Lord, and when pressed, he pays naught, and they beset him.
Is that woeful too? Surely Lord, and when beset,
he pays naught, and they bind him, tie him up.
Is that woeful too? Surely Lord, thus Mangs, poverty, debt, borrowing,
being pressed, beset, and bound are all
woes for an immoral worldly person. Mangs, it is just the same for anyone
who has no faith in wholesome states or skillful states.
It's not conscientious about wholesome states. Has no
fear or blame about wholesome states. No energy for wholesome states. No
insight into wholesome states. He is said to be poor,
needy, in straits, in the Aryan discipline. Now that very man, poor, needy, in
straits, wanting in faith, conscientiousness, fear of blame,
energy, and insight concerning wholesome states,
works evilly in deep word and thought. I call then his getting into debt,
and to cloak his evil deeds, he lays whole upon false hope.
Let none know this of me, he hopes. Let none know this of me, he resolves.
Let none know this of me, he says. Let none know this of me, he strives in act.
So likewise, cloak his evil words, his evil thoughts.
I call then his borrowing. Then his pious fellows in the holy life say thus,
this venerable sir acts in this sort, carries on in this way.
I call then his being pressed. Then gone to forest, tree root, or lonely place.
Evil, unwholesome thoughts, and attendant remorse pursue him.
I call then his being beset. And that man monks, poor, needy, in straits,
having worked evilly in deed, word, and thought,
on the breaking up of the body after death, is bound in hell's bonds,
all the bonds of some animal's womb. And I see no other single bondage, monks,
so harsh, so bitter, such a bar to winning the unsurpassed peace from effort.
I mean hell's bonds, all the bonds of an animal's womb.
That's the end of the sutta. So the Buddha is saying, just as a poor man,
because he is poor, he gets into debt, and then he is being chased by the debtors
for payment, and he cannot pay them, and they tie him up,
then he is in a very sorrowful condition. So in the same way, the Buddha said,
if a person does not know about wholesome states, cannot differentiate between wholesome
and unwholesome states, that means he does not understand the law of karma,
then he is also like that poor man, and he tries to hide his evil actions,
and then other people criticize him, then he's being pressed, being beset,
and even his thoughts, unwholesome thoughts, and thoughts of remorse pursue him,
and that person after debt is born into the woeful plains.
Now the woeful plains are three, the hell is the worst, the second is the animal realm,
and the third is the ghost realm. But here the Buddha says, I see no other single bondage,
monks, so harsh, so bitter, such a bar to winning the unsurpassed peace from effort,
as hell's bonds, or the bonds of an animal's womb. Here the Buddha is saying that when a person
falls into hell, or into the animal realm, that person stays there for a very long time,
a very long time, and because that person has no chance of creating good maritna,
it's very difficult to get out from that who states hell and the animal womb,
and that person stays there for a very long time until the karma, most of that evil karma is worked
out, and then only that person comes up. The next Buddha is 6.5, 0.46. Thus have I heard,
once the venerable Mahajunda lived among the chetis at Sahaja Jati, and there he spoke to the
monks saying, monks, reverensors, reverensor they rejoined, and the venerable Mahajunda said,
there are some monks, dhamma zelots, who blame meditators, saying, these fellows say,
we are meditators, we are meditators, they meditate and are meditated, and what do they
meditate about? What's good of their meditating? How do they meditate? They read neither the dhamma
zelots nor the meditator monks are pleased, nor have they fared onwards for the will of many folk,
for the happiness they're good, nor for the will and happiness of devas and men. I just stopped here
for a while. Here the venerable Mahajunda is saying that there are some monks who are,
what we call, scholar monks, and they criticize meditator monks. And here the word meditator
is jayinna or jayinna, and to meditate is jayati or jayanti. Actually this word jayati or jayanti
literally means to burn, to burn, and another meaning is bright, in conditions, and that is
the word that is used for jana. Jana is a state where the mind becomes one-pointed
and the mind becomes bright. So in the early days of the Buddha or Buddhism during the Buddha's time,
meditation was synonymous to cultivating jana. That is why the word jayati actually means to
cultivate jana. That is why from this you can understand why in the early stages of Buddhism
in China, they had this meditation school which was called the Chan Zhong. Chan Zhong is the Zen
school and this Chan, the word Chinese word Chan comes from the word Chan Noh. It's an abbreviation
of Chan Noh and Chan Noh is the Pali word jana. So in the early stages of Buddhism meditation
is plainly synonymous with cultivating jana. And to continue, the Mahajunda said,
and there are some monks, meditators, who blame dhamma zealots saying, these fellows say, we are
dhamma zealots. We are dhamma zealots. They are puffed up, proud, excitable fellows,
multi-specifiers, forgetful of mindfulness, lacking self-possession and composure with their
thoughts of wonder and their senses uncontrolled. And what is their dhamma zeal? What goods their
dhamma zeal? How are they zealous in dhamma? There too, neither the meditators nor the dhamma zealots
are pleased, nor have they fared onwards for the wheel of many folk. And there are some dhamma
zealots who just praise dhamma zealots, who praise not meditators. There too, neither dhamma zealots
nor meditators are pleased. And so, neither are pleased, nor have they fared onwards for the
wheel of many folk, for their happiness, their good, for the wheel and happiness of devas and men.
Therefore, sirs, you should train yourselves thus, in being dhamma zealots, we will praise meditators.
Thus should you train yourselves, reverences. And why? Fairly, sirs, these wondrous persons
are hardly found in the world. They who live with their whole being in tune with the devilas.
And you should train yourselves thus, reverences. Being meditators, we will praise dhamma zealots.
Thus should you train yourselves, reverences. And why? Fairly, sirs, these wondrous persons are
hardly found in the world. They who with insight penetrate and see the deep way of the goal.
That's the end of the sutta. In this sutta, you find the scholar monks blaming the,
criticizing the meditator monks. And the meditator monks sometimes criticize the
scholarly monks. But if a monk is a well-developed monk, he should both be a scholar monk as well
as a meditator monk. You can see, many of the buddhas, arhans, they knew the sutta very well.
And yet, they were good in their meditation, so that they became arhans. So, now, since
time immemorial, people have been criticizing each other. Even the Buddha was criticized by
external psych-asetics. And the Buddha also criticized external psych-asetics. But in the
Buddha's teachings, the Buddha taught that we should criticize wrong teachings in order to
point out the true teachings. However, we should not get personal. So, we should criticize the
wrong teachings to point out the true teachings. But we should not get personal and criticize
persons. Only the teachings, because if we get personal, then anger arises and unwholesome states
of mind arise. And there will be a lot of problems in the sangha. Now, we come to the next sutta,
6.5.49. Once, when the exalted one was dwelling near Savati at Jeta Grove in Anathapindika
Spa, the venerable Kema and the venerable Sumana dwelt in Andhra Grove near Savati,
and they went and visited the exalted one, saluted him and sat down at one side. So seated,
the venerable Kema said to him, Lord, whatsoever man is arahan, has destroyed the asavas,
lived the life, done what ought to be done, sat down the burden, found the highest goal,
destroyed, becoming spawns, and is in high knowledge released. To him there comes no thought,
there is one better than I, nor there is one equal, nor there is one worse. Thus spoke the
venerable Kema and the master approved, and seeing that the venerable Kema got up, saluted the exalted
one and took his departure, passing him by on the right. Now, not long after he had gone,
the venerable Sumana said this to the exalted one, Lord, whatsoever man is arahan, has destroyed the
asavas, lived the life, done what ought to be done, sat down the burden, found the highest gain,
destroyed, becoming spawns, and is in high knowledge released. To him there comes no thought,
there is none better than I, nor there is none equal, nor there is none worse. Thus spoke the
venerable Sumana and the master approved, and seeing that the venerable Sumana got up, saluted,
and took his departure, passing by on the right. And shortly after they had gone, the exalted one
addressed the man, saying, Thus man's two clansmen declare knowledge. The goal is told, but self is
not mentioned. Yet there are some foolish fellows here who declare knowledge braggingly, it seems,
afterwards they suffer remorse. That's the end of the sutta. This sutta shows how the monks and
nuns during the Buddha's time, when they attained arahanhood, how they came and declared to the
teacher, the Buddha, that they had attained arahanhood. But in an indirect way, they did not say that I
attained arahanhood. And nowadays we see some people who learn some meditation and after a while
they're very eager to let others know that they have become arya. And this shows that they have
not attained arahanhood, especially if they get angry when others don't believe that they have
attained sutta-panna or sakadagami. And this ego just makes it obvious that that person has not
attained. Now we come to the next sutta, 6.5.51. Now the venerable Ananda visited the venerable Sariputta,
greeted him, and after exchanging the usual polite talk, sat down at one side. So seated the
venerable Ananda said this to the venerable Sariputta. How venerable Sariputta? May among
learned new doctrine and doctrines learn, remain unconfused, and all doctrines to which previously
he was mentally attuned remain in use, and he get to know something not known. The venerable Ananda
is very learned. Let the venerable Ananda illuminate this. And the venerable Ananda said,
Well then, reverend Sariputta, listen, pay good heed and I will speak. Yes, sir, he rejoined.
And the venerable Ananda said, Consider reverend Sariputta, among who masters dhamma. As learned,
as mastered, he teaches others dhamma in detail. As learned, as mastered, he makes others say it
in detail. As learned, as mastered, he makes others repeat it in detail. As learned, as mastered,
he ever reflects, ever ponders over it in his mind. Mindfully, he pours on it. Wheresoever,
abide elders, learn it in traditional knowledge. Dhamma-minders, vinaya-minders, epitomists,
there he spends Vasa, the rain season, retreat. And visiting them from time to time, questions
and inquires of them. This talks, sir, what verily is its aim. And their reverences disclose the
undisclosed, make as it were a causeway where there is none. Drive away doubt concerning many perplexing
things. In this way, reverend Sariputta, among may learn new doctrine, and doctrines learned,
remain unconfused. All doctrines to which previously he was mentally attuned, remain in use,
and he gets to know something not known. Excellent, amazing, reverend sir,
is all this that has been so well said by the venerable Ananda. We will mind that the venerable
one is endowed with these six things. The venerable Ananda masters dhamma. The venerable Ananda
teaches others dhamma in detail, as learned, as mastered. The venerable Ananda makes others say
it. The venerable Ananda makes others repeat it. The venerable Ananda reflects ponders,
wars on it. Wheresoever learned, elders abide. There the venerable Ananda spends Vasa,
and he questions them. This talks, sir, what is its aim. And they disclose the undisclosed,
make a causeway where there is none. Drive away doubt concerning many perplexing things.
That's the end of the sutta. This is another sutta which shows the wisdom of venerable Ananda.
At that time, the venerable Ananda was only a sota-panna, while the venerable Sariputta was
already an arahan. And yet, the venerable Ananda still has one or two things that he could do
and arahan. And it's not that the venerable sutta is less wise because the venerable Sariputta
is said to be the highest in wisdom. But as we learned in one of the earlier suttas, the Buddha
said it is hard to find somebody equal in wisdom to the venerable Ananda. The venerable Ananda
is said to be the top as far as the most learned as far as the suttas, the teachings of the Buddha.
Our concern, he could memorize, he could remember all the suttas taught by the Buddha.
And the sutta, there are six things that are said to be good for a monk to practice.
First, to master dhamma, to teach others dhamma in detail, to make others say it in detail,
to make others repeat it in detail, to ever ponder over it, and to spend vatsa where there are learned
monks. In exuta 6.5.53, then another Brahmin visited the exalted one, greeted him, exchanged
compliments and sat down at one side. So seated, he said, Master Gautama, is there one thing which
when made become, made to increase, embraces and establishes, two wheels wheel here and wheel here
after? There is Brahmin. What is it, Master Gautama? Verily, earnestness Brahmin is the one
thing which when made become, made to increase, embraces and establishes, two wheels wheel here
and wheel here after. Brahmin, as the footmarks of all prowling creatures are admitted in an
elephants, and the elephants foot is counted chief. So earnestness is the one thing which when made
become, made to increase, embraces and establishes, two wheels wheel here and wheel here after.
As all rafters in a peaked house reach to the peak, slope to the peak, unite in the peak,
and the peak is counted chief. So earnestness Brahmin is the one thing which when made become,
made to increase, embraces and establishes, two wheels wheel here and wheel here after.
As the light of the stars is not worth a sixteenth part, the light of the moon,
and the light of the moon is counted chief. So earnestness Brahmin is the one thing which
when made become, made to increase, embraces and establishes, two wheels wheel here and wheel here
after, as the end of the sutta. This word earnestness is the Pali word apamada,
sometimes translated as diligence or sincerity, and it means doing what needs to be done
in the spiritual path. One has to be careful with half measures that is compromising,
that means not being earnest or sincere enough. For example, some persons want to practice the
holy life and also want to get married. And if they do get married, this shows a lack of earnestness
and here that person ends up being neither successful in this spiritual path or neither does
he become a good husband or a good father. If we are earnest or sincere, we obtain happiness in
this life and happiness hereafter. And we should note that when we practice the spiritual path
correctly, we should be happy, not sad, not as some people who think that because they are
contemplating dukkha all the time, they should be full of dukkha. But in the Dhamma-cetiya sutta
and the Majjhima Nikaya sutta number 89, King Pasanadi Kozala came to visit the Buddha and
kissed his feet. This is an Indian custom which you find also in Sri Lanka where lay people kiss
the monk's feet. And the Buddha asked the king, why did he pay so much homage to this body?
And the king started to praise the Buddha, all the qualities of the Buddha and also the monks
under the Buddha. And the king said that whereas in other external ascetics, you could see that
they were lean and wretched looking, looking very miserable and unhappy, whereas the monks
under the Buddha, they looked very happy and exceedingly happy and joyful. And so we should
note that if we practice the holy path and we obtain the bliss of renunciation, the happiness
of renunciation, we should be happy and looking happy. And the next sutta is 6.5.54. Now Brahmin
Janusoni approached the exalted one, greeted him, and after exchanging the usual polite talk,
sat down at one side. So seated he said, Master Gautama, what's a noble's aim? What's his quest?
What's his resolve? What's his want? What's his ideal? I'll just stop here for a moment to explain.
His noble is a member of the warrior caste. In Pali, it's Katiya. In Sanskrit, it's Shatriya,
one of the four castes in India. Now the sutta continues, Brahmin, whilst a noble's aim,
wisdom's his quest, powers his resolve, the earth's his want, dominion's his ideal,
and the Brahmin asked again, and the Brahmin's Master Gautama, what's his aim? His quest, etc.
And the Buddha said, whilst his aim, wisdom's his quest, mantras are his resolve, sacrifices his
want, Brahma's world is his ideal. Again the Brahmin asked, and a householder, what's his
aim, his quest, etc., Master Gautama, and the Buddha said, whilst his aim, wisdom's his quest,
crafts his resolve, works his want, works and is his ideal. Again the Brahmin asked, and a woman's
Master Gautama, what's her aim, her quest, etc., the Buddha said, man's her aim, adornments her
quest, a son's her resolve, to be without a rival is her want, dominion is her ideal,
and a thief's aim, a thief's quest, etc., Master Gautama,
Buddhist's his aim, captures his quest, a caravan's his resolve, darkness is his want,
not to be seen is his ideal, and a recluse, Master Gautama, what's his aim, his quest, etc.,
patience and forbearance are his aim, wisdom's his quest, virtues his resolve, nothing is his
want, nibbana is his ideal, wonderful, wonderful Master Gautama, verily Master Gautama knows the
aim, the quest, the resolve, the want, the ideal of nobles, of Brahmins, householders, women,
thieves and recluses, this is indeed wonderful Master Gautama, and so the Brahmin took refuge
in the Buddha and said, from henceforth till life ends, I will go to the Master Gautama for help,
and that's the end of the Sutta. This is an interesting Sutta, I shows the
witness of the Buddha, the ability of the Buddha to answer questions, and you can see
the answers he gave are quite interesting. As far as monks are concerned, the things mentioned by
the Buddha are of interest to a monk, patience and forbearance are a monk's aim, wisdom's a monk's
quest, virtues his resolve, nothing is his want, nibbana is his ideal.
